You're in for a treat today. This is the first time we've done this. It was interesting yesterday on my flight. I'm sure a lot of you either snooze or read or do something. I always read. So a lot of times when I speak from the stage I'll teach the concept of personal hooks. It's you know I try to tell people as many things about me as simply as possible to talk about my kids talk about But back here I'm talking about when I'm from talk about music talk about whatever it is because I want as many people in the audience It's possible to say that guy's just like me. I like that TV show too, or I've got kids too I've got you know You're not going to get everybody But you want to try to get as many people to feel like they have as many things in common with you as possible because Affinity is what builds a trust and if if if you have a boat too and I had a boat then You like me because I approve of you having a boat right because you have a boat too you're pouring money in the water too, right? All right, so let's do a case study Burger King You can actually violate your own brand story and turn it into your your own advantage and Burger King did this well And that's if your objective is to remind people how much they love your story So if your objective is to remind people how much they love your story you can actually violate it in order to catch them off Guard so Burger King's brand story is what it's the home of the Whopper, right? I mean that's been Burger King forever But they purposely killed that brand in one restaurant and filmed it just to prove how powerful it really is and it resulted in a Ward winning ad campaign. We want to make it so that if our story was taken out of the equation people would be pissed right? They'd be upset they because we are that valuable to them. They're that emotionally attached to us All right, so we're gonna do it. It's called kill your brand All right your brand died October 2012. So many someone hopefully will miss it. This is just an exercise Don't worry. No, no actual harm will come to your brand So I want you to imagine that your brand died right as if we're a person that had a funeral How would it be remembered? How would it be written up? What this tells you is is your story effective? This is what this exercise will tell you would your brand obituary be like a man who literally changed the way we live our lives every day Steve Jobs the visionary founder and leader of Apple Computer has died at the age of 56 a giant in business Technology and entertainment his place in history was assured when he helped invent the personal computer, but that Was only the beginning ten years Apple had grown from just the two of us in a garage Into a two billion dollar company with over 4,000 employees His myth grew into Thomas Edison meets Willy Wonker proportions a titan of American business Steve Jobs was just 56 years old Or would it be like this? Hello, I'm Kerry Dolan in Silicon Valley on the forbes calm video network blockbuster shares tumble 27% to 40 cents a share After the bell following news that the company might have to file for bankruptcy The video rental chain is trying to whittle down a load of debt blockbuster reported a fourth-quarter loss of 435 million dollars and the company is a shadow of its former self Yeah, the brutal huh, and I think most of us pride this this news report Damon Cross our past because no one gives a Damn about blockbuster at all. I mean so irrelevant to the conversation Completely irrelevant that you know no one cares. Alright, so Why don't you go ahead and write your brand obituary right now? What would your customers say would they really miss your brand would they feel positively about your brand or would it not really make Them blank What would your competitors say would they be relieved? Thank God, that's a guy who that guy was killing me right or would they just struggle because your brand really wasn't a threat to theirs So this is important exercise because it forced you to look at what your brand story is at this minute If it's not powerful if it doesn't trigger anyone's love hormone your brand has a big problem All right, so we're gonna I'm gonna give you five minutes to try to literally try to write the obituary for your brand If you're out of business tomorrow, what would it say in the newspaper? It's a high level strategy that only delivers high level results for big companies, but also our clients. I'll show you some examples. We're doing a lot of it. And it wasn't until last year that we really found the most powerful way of story selling. And that was with Jacob's turn, and that was because I got sold on a story. Those of you who don't know the story, I was in Chicago's Midway Airport going somewhere, and there's another guy going somewhere else. And that's what happens in airports, I guess. But neither of our final destinations, I sat down next to a guy. He flipped over in his laptop to start working, and I was going to do some work, whatever to. I had a little picture of a little boy smiling on the baseball bat. It says, that's your son. He said, yeah, it is. I said, he's a beautiful kid. He said, thanks so much. He has Down syndrome. I said, well, yeah, I figured. I have a couple family members with Down syndrome. You can kind of tell what the characteristics. And you got to start talking. He said, yeah, it was such a blessing to our family. And it was his youngest son. His wife had a baby after 40. He had a typical scenario for that. And so we had talking, and he found out kind of what I did. And he said, hey, you know, would you mind? Because this guy's name is Jim Titus. And Jim Titus never met someone who wasn't a friend. I mean, like, instantly. This guy can connect fast. And he's like, hey, you know, we have a Down syndrome. He's from Floyd's Knob's, Indiana. And we have the Down syndrome of Lower Eastern, Upper Louisiana, or Indiana, whatever has this gale every year. And we have a charity auction with celebrity memorabilia. You said you do entertainment stuff. Could you get me some stuff? I'm like, yeah, sure. I can use my email, you know? Email me. Because I don't ever mind trying to do that stuff. It's actually, it's funny, charity auctions usually go horribly wrong or not well, because most people don't value the things that you value, and they're really hard to get. Right? And there's not usually enough people who really want something in a room that they're going to fight for it. But anyway, but I don't mind doing it. But most people never will email you back or call you or whatever. So I gave him my stuff, and he emailed back straight up, and I got him some rascal flat stuff and what I got him some stuff, sent it on. And then like six months later, he was like, hey, my wife just wrote this story. It hadn't been published in the paper yet. Don't share it with anyone. Well, I didn't, I mean, I'm not sharing one. So I just, I read it and I started crying. I was like, this man, this is a good story. So I just shared it with Jack and my dad and just say, hey, you guys, you know, this is a cool story. Check it out. And then they don't even know 100 people each. And I swear each of them forwarded to 200 people each. I'm like, you, I didn't tell you not to share it because you don't have any friends. But that's how I'm like, this story is viral. All right? Like instantly this story has got to be told more than in the Floyd's knobs, Gazette or whatever it's going is going something local newspaper. So what happens is we had a mastermind meeting actually in this hotel is for the Grammys three years ago now and a great room with, you know, Allie Brown and Mark Smith. We just bet Dave is lots of really smart people. And I, I sat in front of him and said, Hey, for my mastermind time, I'm just going to read you this story. So we read you the story. I read the story. Everyone's crying. Not a damn one of them not crying. Don't let them tell you they weren't. Guys, all the women, everybody crying. So here's the deal. I want to go tell this story. I'm going to go make a little movie on it. I've never done that before. Just got to trust me. And I need to raise the money. So here's what I'm going to do. I'm going to give you value, make you a producer, make you an executive producer based on the amount you donate. I'm going to go tell the story. And so got enough commitments. Everybody did it. We went and told the story. We ended up winning Emmy with it. It was pretty cool. And if you haven't seen it, this is a short clip of preview of the clip. This is Floyd Snobbs, Indiana. Around here, life lessons are learned at home, church, and on the baseball field. We all go through a hard-witted time, so we're looking forward to baseball and warm weather's around, flowers blooming. It's kind of like the Kentucky Derby. Like most around here, my whole family plays. Lauren, my oldest, has been playing for five seasons. Matthew, who's nine for four, but my youngest. It was going to be his first season, and I was anxious. What would the coaches think? How would the parents react? What would the kids say? Would they tease him? I didn't know if he could handle that. I didn't know if I could handle that. You see, Jacob has Down syndrome. I'm not sure that Jacob's not the normal one, and the rest of us are abnormal. So that was a little trailer we put together for that, and just ended up being a very cool. It kind of changed a big part of what I was doing in life in general, just started making movies and stuff. And it was an incredible way to bring his story to life, and the effect it had on viewers was equally incredible. It changed a lot of people's hearts. That's when we saw how awesome the power of a film could be. So that's when we really focused on... I always knew that film, and we had done some infomercial work in the past, I knew that moving pictures and music and stories were the most manipulative form of media on earth. We could not know anything about someone watching a movie like Fry Green Tomatoes or Beaches or something. I never even seen that one, but I hear you cry. And so you could cry for people who don't even exist and you do not know. That is powerful. So imagine what you can do with that with a purpose to sell. So actually my main goal as Jack in our Brainstorm was to actually build the first movie studio for Markers, and that's really kind of what we're working on. So just like Fox has reality shows, they have commercials, they do infomercials, they do feature films, they do sitcoms. I want to do the same exact thing, but I want to do it all with the purpose of pushing product, and obviously only with good product and good things people sell, because you can use that manipulation for good or for evil. I like to use it for good. So and that inspired another big idea. What if film could be used to bring brand stories of our clients to life? What if we could involve their customers in those stories and hit that love hormone and really showcase what was special about them? So we began making branded business films last year and we've all been blown away by the results. But let's get back to you and your business. You've seen how and why stories work the way they do. So a lot of this first part has been a lot of the psychology of why this works. So to convince you over and over and over and over again to show you that this does work, that you should use it, your business is not different, it will work. And with your own obituary, you've taken a look at your own brand story and how it stands today. How can your story sell yourself? That's really the crux of this whole deal. If you don't have an answer, you're denying yourself the single most powerful way to make your brand memorable and powerful. And many of you probably don't think you have a great story. You're wrong. But that says you're wrong. All right. There's something in your life or business that makes you unique, that makes you stand out, that differentiates you from the rest of the pack. And that's where I like to do what I call the $10,000 test. So everyone's going to write this question down and if you win, I will give you $10,000. This is really true. So pay attention. I want you to write down the name of the city and state you were born in. Write down your favorite teacher in high school. So here's a little longer than others. Write down the name of the person you shared your first kiss with. Don't worry. We'll burn these. This is all the fun questions, too. And write if your first kiss was standing up or laying down. And then the last thing, write down the name of the last movie you saw that you loved. That you saw that you loved. You'd actually tell people to go watch it. All right. Now here's a $10,000 test. If any of those match, if any two people in the room match, I'll give you each $10,000. They don't match at all because we all have a unique set of circumstances that brought us to where we are in our life. Now I could have done it business-wise, but it's much more fun to do the kissing, standing up, laying down deal. So I won't make you share those. You can cross them out. Do whatever you like. Burn them. But the whole point is that you have a story that's interesting to people because, I mean, interestingly, from doing the movies we were doing right now, the documentaries, I mean, I've done three of them on tax lawyers. And I love them to death, but tax law is not the most interesting thing on earth. And they're actually really interesting and they will make you cry. I mean, these movies will make you cry just because some of the stories were telling of the clients who lost their house in a tornado and they owed tax money. I mean, just there's a story in everything. You just ain't digging deep enough if you don't know what it is. All right. The secret Hollywood formula to make sure your story resonates with people. Now this is the deal. This is like the most important thing. It's the formula behind it all. To create the right plot for your brand and to frame it in the most impactful way possible. There's some common narratives that affect, that all effective stories share. And it's all based off of this book. This guy probably does it most distinctly. The Seven Basic Plots, Why We Tell Stories by Christopher Booker. The good news is I'm not going to make you labor through this. We're going to take a break right now. We're going to spend the second half of this morning going through the seven major plot lines and how you can integrate them into your business. Sound good? Perfect. All right, cool. Let's take a 15 minute break and we'll come back. So a quarter till whatever time it is on the west coast. Let's go 10.45. 10.45. Cool. All right. Hello. All right. So we are going to get cranking here again. So we're about to get into the seven basic plots, which is what's really going to break down for you and make things make more sense. All right, cool. These are the plots that repeat time and time again throughout human history. And so there are something like 60 something sub plots, major sub plots, but the seven major plots is really all you need to focus on because they best represent human experience. And at the end of the day, again, common experience is what bonds people. And audiences are constantly drawn to these seven plots over and over and over and over and over again. I mean, it's just what it is. So you've got to apply, you've got to, wow, that's great. What you got to do is apply these proven plot lines to your branding. That was one of them. You got to apply these proven plot lines to your branding. So you hit a primal nerve that will get you the response you're looking for. So it's all about hitting this, striking the nerve, making it happen. All right. Let's start with the first basic plot. Frankenstein, the alien from well, alien Darth Vader from Star Wars. All three of these figures prominently, figure prominently into our first basic plot, which is overcoming the monster. All right. Now those are some pretty obvious monsters, but there are lots of other monsters that aren't as obvious. So again, plot number one is overcoming the monster. And let's take a look at this clip from a recent movie. There is carnage on Wall Street. The shares of the major financial stocks plummeting. It's triggering a major sell-off in the markets. The damage widespread. The Aztecs plunging, the Dow Jones industrial averages plunging, and oil is plunging. You can hear the hollering on the floor. We're down to 60. Literally a split second. The Dow Jones industrial average now 500 points on the decline. The headline of the new housing report is grim, but this is shaping up to be the worst real estate market in a generation. We are now at the highest point, 724. That was the biggest loss we've seen today. That is, we're now in historic territory. Investors are getting their heads handed to the loop as another U.S. banking institution nears collapse. Now the President's working group is going into emergency meetings with the Secretary of the Treasury and the Federal Reserve Board. The President's Chief of Staff is calling back in an hour. He's with the President. He's going to want to know what I think we should do. This is in Keller's able, Bill. This is too big to fail. This is just an insane situation. This is now. There's about 70 plus trillion out there in credit default swaps, held by roughly 17 banks, and we do not know where the bottom is. All right, so that's from the remake of Wall Street. The shock of that moment is just as scary as the alien coming out of a guy's stomach, right? I mean, it's just, yeah, or worse. It's financially to many people, much worse. And in this case, the 2008 economic meltdown was the monster that had to be overcome, along, of course, with Gordon Gekko, right? So great movie if you haven't seen it. Very well done. Everyone has a personal monster, everyone. We all have monsters. We have to overcome in our lives. By portraying that battle and showing how we courageously stopped that monster, we create a memorable branding scenario. That's important. Showing how we overcame that monster, it creates a memorable scenario for those who are working with. Some common monsters are poverty, a brutal competitor, drug addiction, debt, all very common monsters that many people have fought. These are just a few of the examples of the monsters some people must battle in order to succeed. I'm going to show you a clip from one of our clients, Bart Queen. This is one of the first movies. I think this is the first movie we ever shot. He battled for years with a very real monster, which was his own fear. I think there are four types of people that really kind of live their lives. Group number one are those people who never even want to find their purpose. You know what? They get up, they go to work, they do their thing, they're going to live and they're going to die and they're going to go from zero to sixteen. It doesn't even come across their mind. The second group of people are those who seek their purpose. I mean, they sincerely want to live that out and they never find it. The third group of people are those people who find their purpose and they live it out. And whether that's changing the world in some way, whether it's being a great mom or dad, it doesn't matter. And the last group are those who seek it. They find it, but they live in fear. I know that one because that's really my story. Fifty years of living in fear and not following the path, it's the most painful thing I think you can do. I'm hoping to help other people avoid my pain. And the only way to do that is share my pain, share my story, live out that path and help someone else that's on the same path. So that's Bart's story. So Bart, great guy from North Carolina, Bart's gay and he didn't let anyone know for years and years and years and years and years and years. And his whole business is about helping people live their life purposefully. And so that's a big part of his story. You know, I always tell people, it's interesting too, I really kind of first realized the thing when I was working on Bart's movie. We all have these stories in our past, these things that have just haunted us. That failure, we had that miserable dismal failure that we want to do whatever, we just want to shut the door on that thing, right? And then we go, we have another thing that we don't manage, rejection, someone wanted to shut the door on that. And so we end up with these skeletons in our closet that are deep, right? There's a deep closet with lots of doors. And I think it is not until you're willing to open every one of those doors does your life actually flow effortlessly. Because everything's come through those doors. All those lessons, all those skeletons, that's what people will actually connect with the most. And the less you try to hide them, the more you can be who you are. Because all of those moments, like the $10,000 challenge, all those moments added up to who you are today. So it makes you an actual multi-dimensional person. But it's just an interesting way that all kind of shakes out. You know, the viewer can't help but get caught up in an overcoming the monster plot, right? And particularly in this one, right? I mean, the stakes are high, the outcome's uncertain, and the conflict is epic. I mean, you're talking about an internal conflict. Someone cannot pass, go, cannot collect $200, right? Until they've, he was teaching people how to live their life purpose in line to everyone and himself most importantly about who he was, right? So that's an interesting conflict, right? And he has 13 Clydesdale horses, which makes for beautiful footage, right? All right. Let's move on to our second basic class. So overcoming the monster. Everyone get that one? Makes sense? All right, cool. Scarface, Rocky, and apropos for the day, Pretty Woman with Julia Roberts. What could these three movies possibly have in common? Scarface, Rocky, and Pretty Woman, right? They're all prime examples of plot number two, which is rags to riches, okay? Very common plot, rags to riches. Al Pacino is a broke human immigrant who became a criminal. Rocky was a guy breaking the thumbs of dead beats who had monsters when he got a shot at fighting the heavyweight champ. And Julia Roberts was a woman of the evening who suddenly became the toast of the town thanks to Richard Gere, right? Everyone loved the great success story and sometimes they even won Oscars, right? Calls are just listed from Mumbai. Chaiwala. The two crore, 20 million rupees. Ramis. Which is, I have to tell you, regret Leave the Sock. The Red Sock. Go on and set off, buddy! Great movie, you guys haven't seen that Slipknot Millionaire. I believe it was made for like a million bucks or something. It was made for really cheap and won a lot of awards. The appeal of the Rags to Richard story is pretty obvious, right? It's the story of the American Dream. That's really the bottom line of what that story is. You can come from absolutely nothing and get to the top. Who does not like hearing that? We all felt no matter where we came from, we were nowhere at some point in our life, right? And if you're feeling that way, particularly at the moment, it's very apropos. And if that is success, it's earned, people applaud you for it. So if you found your way out, that's what they want to hear. Especially if the audience feels that you've overcome incredible odds. I mean, that's like, they then start rooting for you, like in that movie. You don't even know this guy, he's a fake guy, but you're rooting for that guy, right? And you would cry if something bad happened to him. He'll be in your corner forever, like someone about a millionaire. And if you have this Rags to Richard's component to your story, it's awesome to use it. I mean, use it, you know, in all these things, you have to be tasteful, tactful. I mean, that goes without saying, but there's a great way to use that stuff. Here's one with a familiar face. As I was finishing up undergrad, I met this amazing girl, Christina, who became my wife. And we actually got married after our first semester of law school. She was a nurse, working hard as a nurse. She helped put me through law school, and I can never thank her enough for that. Then I finished law school, and my first son was born a week after I took the bar. So here's what I knew coming out of law school. I had over $100,000 in student loan debt. I knew I wanted to help a lot of people. I knew I loved entertainment and music, but I knew I had to make a lot of money to pay for, not only, you know, the bills I'd racked up in law school, but now I had a family. I had a family to take care of. So I asked Jack, what should I do? And he said, you know, Nick, you have a skill set, a distinct skill set in media creation. You have learned from the best people in the industry this kind of celebrity buzz building stuff. If we take my concept of people by people, my skill set in personal branding and direct marketing, and we combine that with your skill set of media creation and celebrity buzz building, he said, I think we can absolutely change the game. Why don't we build you as one of the top entertainment attorneys in the country? I think we can build you credibility pretty quickly. I think you can, you know, make a life for your family. So that's from our movie. We're almost done with it. It's actually being finished right now. So that's part of my story, right? So many entrepreneurs and professionals come from humble beginnings. I mean, a lot of people do in life, and most everybody is not born with silver spoons in their mouth. And it's not something to be ashamed of, right? It's who you are. It makes you who you are, and it makes a lot, a lot of people connect with you. Particularly the more successful you are, especially if you're trying to lead other people on that path, you know, any sort of, I can take you there too. The more you show them that, you're just like them. The more it works. The typical story, origin story for like an infomercial is this. We'll use a weight loss example. I'm Nick Nanton. I weigh 90 pounds, so can what? Not true, but this is all fictionalized, okay? I weigh 90 pounds, so can what? But it wasn't always that way. I used to weigh 1800 pounds. Used to have to have a crane come get me out of the house every day, knock down the wall, blah, blah, blah. Until I discovered this new Margarita shake, and now I weigh 90 pounds, and I can show you how to. So it's, here's who I am today, and I am the epitomical of what you want to be. But it wasn't always that way. I used to be just like you. And this is the self-help and the speaking business. It's the only business in the world that the more screwed up you had it in the past, the more money you're going to make. Right? That's just the way it works. So it wasn't always that way. So you brought the glasses back on and struggled with that. It's interesting, isn't it? Yeah. Some of those pictures, I didn't have glasses. This is a long time ago, right? You can't control where you come from just where you go. I'm like, no, this is part of my brand, man. It's way more interesting, you know? And if you went far, it's impressive to a wide audience, right? Quests are compelling to people because they seem insanely difficult if not impossible, which explains the success of this huge film franchise, mission impossible. And then there's also Cartman. Tracy Myers, a lot of you will meet this weekend, was on his own kind of Moneyball quest when he took over as dad's dealership. The car lot had way too much turnover in the sales department, which is typical in a car dealership. And so Tracy had to find a way to fix the situation. So I'll show you a clip from his movie here. When I saw the turnover was so high, not just at our dealership, but in the used car industry, new car industry in general, there had to be a way to keep people in the business longer. We would always ask, well, why are you leaving? And they would all say, well, I can't work on this commission. That's the way they did it. They did put them on commission. You don't sell, you don't get any money. I came up with a non-commissioned sales professional pay plan, which was a small salary, and bonuses based on complete customer satisfaction and units, so to speak. And I presented that to my dad and he said, this is the kiss of death. I'm telling you, this will not work. It was quite a battle to get that to happen. We went through about two years back and forth when he owned the dealership, and the first thing I did when I bought the dealerships, I changed the pay plan. And they've tweaked it several different ways, both of them together. An amazing thing happened, the sales staff turnover almost went away overnight. Doing away with the commission, making everybody like a family and making them feel secure, having health insurance, and making a decent paycheck every week. That's a good way to do it. Tracy's a trend setter. He does things that other people are scared to do. Cool. There's that clip there. So interestingly, this is a story about a used car dealer, which is intriguing, and it's interesting. And then the other one was about a speaking coach, and we're running for four Amis this year in this stuff. This is to tell you that stories that you wouldn't know are there, you can uncover them. And one of the biggest hits of all time, Titanic. Plot number six is tragedy. All of these movies end with death and loss. And it involved very bad things happening to the heroes. Marlon Brando and Al Pacino both end up dead in the Godfather saga. Jack Nicholson gets a lobotomy in cuckoo's nest. And Leo de Caprio freezes to death in the ocean. All right. Tragedy of all of our plots evokes the most powerful emotional reaction of any one of them. You might recognize a clip. All right. Dad, why are you still in Hallelujah? How should I know? What do you think I am? Addictionary? Tommy, stop that. Stop it. Jenny, haven't you learned that silly tune yet? You're playing over and over again. Now stop it. Stop it. You're playing over and over again. Now stop it. Stop it. Hey, what's the matter with you? Look where you're going. I'm going to kill you. I'm going to kill you. I'm going to kill you. All right. So it's a wonderful life. In this particular scene, it's not such a wonderful life. Jimmy Stewart, his business just crashed due to an absent-minded uncle and a ruthless banker. So he starts thinking suicide is the only way out, right? Sounds like some fun branding, right? Well, it can be. Think about the celebrities who opened up to Barbara Walters and People Magazine about things like the horrible past, drug addiction, and alcohol abuse, horrible relationships, and so on and so on and so on. And I think about how many times in those cases that has revitalized their entire career, right? It just allowed people to connect with them again. Tragedy in any story creates sympathy and emotional bonding. So that's what tragedy creates. Sympathy and emotional bonding, two essential elements of storytelling or sorry, of storytelling. Here's another clip from Rob Russell's movie where we use tragedy. My husband did all of our financial advising and coordination and loved date trading. Just adored it. And he tried very hard to get me interested. I had no interest whatsoever. So he said if anything ever happened to him, I needed to find a financial advisor. And something happened to him. He died suddenly. And so I took his advice, find someone who could take care of me. So I called and I was fortunate enough to get Rob. And so they adopted me. Everyone experiences loss and everyone has very unhappy periods in their lives, right? We've all been there. Using tragedy in a story makes you more sympathetic and relatable. And again, building trust through sympathy but being relatable. You're a real person just like them. Putting your pants one leg at a time. It's another extension of that, right? If everything goes great, there's no real conflict and there's nothing interesting to focus on. The first thing we try to do anytime we do any sort of film, documentary, movie, anybody's try to figure out what went wrong somewhere. That's usually it. Yours is a little different. But just we try to find some sort of tragedy or what didn't work out or whatever else. Because yours is about having a party. When you talk about tragedy, the audience wants to know how you dealt with it and they want to compare notes. That's at the end of the day, man, how do they deal with that? Because I know that's going to hit me at some point, you know? I'm always intrigued by how people deal with espouse or themselves getting cancer. Just because it's just such a reality and life we live in today that someone close to you is going to get cancer, right? I mean, just what it is. Now, drumroll please, let's move on to our seventh and final basic plot. So that was tragedy. Christmas Carol, Batman Begins, Lion King, Rebirth. Plot number seven, Rebirth. Rebirth is our seventh and final basic plot line. Think of Scrooge behind the crutches at Turkey at the end of Christmas Carol. Bruce Wayne losing his parents traveling to Asia and being trained to be reborn as Batman. Simba losing his father and finally regaining his rightful place as the king of the Pride Lands. Rebirth is about overcoming tragedy in a powerful way that inspires the audience and gives them hope. If you can inspire them and give them hope, you're in a pretty good spot, right? So you've got to have tragedy in order to have a rebirth. Everybody loves to come back because it makes them feel that no matter what happens, you can still wind up a winner. But after those trials, we all rallied around what was right and acted as one. Because that's what we do. We find a way through tough times and if we can't find a way, then we'll make one. Again, genuine rebirth requires prior tragedy. Now that might leave you a little confused. Does something like it's a wonderful life fall into the category of tragedy or rebirth? After all, it needs one to achieve the other. Well, here's a little surprise for you. I've added my own eighth basic plot to the seven we've already discussed and that's all the other seven together. The most effective story-selling actually uses elements of all the other seven plot lines we've discussed. Already discussed how it's a wonderful life has tragedy and rebirth. And that's what we're gonna do next. We're gonna do a little bit of a review of the seven plot lines. We've discussed already discussed how it's a wonderful life has tragedy and rebirth. But hey, it's got the other five, too. Here's plot number one. Overcoming the monster happens to be an old man potter, the greedy banker. Here's plot number two. Rags to riches where Jimmy Stewart helps poor people buy their own homes. Plot number three. The quest, Clarence the Angel has returned to Earth to help Jimmy Stewart and earn his wings and frankly has no idea what he's doing. Which means he has to accomplish plot number four, the voyage. He has to leave heaven, come to Earth and fix things. And we certainly have plot number five. Comedy, when everyone who loves this movie loves the scene. We're at the high school dance. The floor opens. Everyone falls in the swimming pool. We'll trip you for you. The director, Frank Kapper, thought up that on the spot when he found out that the school where he was filming had that floor that did that. So he just figured, why not? Most of the biggest movies of all time contain elements of all seven plots. Everything from Gone with the Wind to the Avengers features these story lines to ensure that its interest is high. And all the movies we've talked about in this presentation have several of these plots present. Scarface isn't just Rags Riches. It's also a monster who has to be overcome. He made a voyage into the drug trade in Miami and ends up creating incredible tragedy. Star Wars isn't just about overcoming a monster. It's also a quest, a voyage, and it has tragedy, comedy, and rebirth. In our brand of films we also use multiple plot points from the main seven. You've already seen a couple we used in Carmen. Will we do the same thing in our other stories? It's what we try to do. These are the seven elements that people respond to, the things that make their love hormones hearts beat faster. And again, that's what we want to focus on. And now which of these can you put into your brand story? That's really the question for you. Story seven, seven ways to bring it to brand. The great thing about story selling is you can keep selling that story over and over again, like we talked about, that are useful over and over again in multiple situations. Stories never go out of style and they always attract an audience. That's helpful. You need audiences to sell. And when you bring your brand story to life, you take your business to a completely different level. I'm sure you can see. Now, even though you haven't met a lot of these people I talked about, when you see them these next few days, you know them, right? You feel like you know them. You're going to trust them way more because you know them. It's just what it is, right? Your customers react to you in a positive emotional level. And as we all know, most people buy based on emotions and they buy from people they like. And so the whole point of these is to give them to do what Jack Sells taught me about, no liking trust. If you can accomplish that, you win, right?
